1:
"Now these are the generations of the sons of Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth: and unto them were sons born after the flood." The witnesses note the heading of the generations of Noah's sons. On "these are the generations of the sons of Noah," the grammatical voices note the Table of Nations. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "these are the generations (toledoth) of the sons of Noah" — the formula introduces the genealogy of the nations (the "Table of Nations," ch. 10); the chapter traces the descendants of Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and the peoples that sprang from them; the record of the origin of all the nations from Noah's three sons.

On "Shem, Ham, and Japheth," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the three sons. Henry and Calvin note that the three sons of Noah are the heads of the post-flood race; from them the whole earth was peopled (9:19); the genealogy shows how the nations descended from the three and spread over the earth; the unity of mankind in their descent from Noah, and the diversity of the nations.

On "unto them were sons born after the flood," the grammatical-historical note the new beginning: "unto them were sons born after the flood" — the descendants of the three sons were born after the flood, in the renewed world; the multiplying of mankind (9:1, 7) issued in the nations of the Table; the peopling of the earth from Noah's family; the Table records the fulfillment of the command to be fruitful and replenish the earth. The verse displays the heading of the generations of Noah's sons.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the heading — "these are the generations of the sons of Noah," the Table of Nations introduced (toledoth, Keil, Ellicott); "Shem, Ham, and Japheth," the three sons, the heads of the post-flood race (9:19, Henry, Calvin); "unto them were sons born after the flood," the descendants born in the renewed world (9:1, 7, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the genealogy-heading. The verse marks the generations: of Noah's sons. The verse displays the sons: three. The verse displays the flood: after it. The verse displays the nations: descending. The heading displays the origin of the nations from Noah's three sons. The verse displays the toledoth: of the sons. The verse displays the race: renewed. The verse displays the earth: peopled. The heading of the generations of Noah's sons displays the Table of Nations: these are the generations of Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and unto them were sons born after the flood — the record of the origin of all the nations from Noah's three sons, the peopling of the renewed earth. The verse displays the generations, beginning with Japheth (v.2).
2:
"The sons of Japheth; Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras." The witnesses note the sons of Japheth, the heads of the northern and western nations. On "The sons of Japheth," the grammatical voices note the line of Japheth. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the sons of Japheth; Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras" — the seven sons of Japheth are named first; from them descended the peoples of the north and west (Europe and northern Asia); the genealogy begins with Japheth, the most widely spread (9:27).

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the nations. Clarke and Gill note the peoples descended from Japheth's sons (as identified by the ancient writers): Gomer (the Cimmerians, and the Celtic/Galatian peoples); Magog (the Scythians); Madai (the Medes); Javan (the Ionians/Greeks); Tubal and Meshech (peoples of Asia Minor, the Tibareni and Moschi); Tiras (the Thracians); the heads of the northern and western nations.

On the sons of Japheth, the grammatical-historical note the spread: from these seven sons came the great body of the Gentile nations of Europe and the north; the enlargement of Japheth (9:27) is seen in the wide spread of his descendants; the peoples of the isles of the Gentiles (v.5); the origin of the northern and western nations traced to Japheth. The verse displays the sons of Japheth, the heads of the northern and western nations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "the sons of Japheth; Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras," the seven sons of Japheth (Keil, Ellicott); the peoples descended — Gomer (Cimmerians/Celts), Magog (Scythians), Madai (Medes), Javan (Greeks), Tubal and Meshech (Asia Minor), Tiras (Thracians) (Clarke, Gill); the wide spread of Japheth's descendants (9:27, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Japheth's line. The verse marks the sons: seven. The verse displays the nations: northern and western. The verse displays the Greeks: Javan. The verse displays the Medes: Madai. The genealogy displays the heads of the Gentile nations of the north. The verse displays the Japheth: his sons. The verse displays the peoples: descended. The verse displays the spread: wide. The sons of Japheth, the heads of the northern and western nations, display the enlargement of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras — the seven sons from whom the peoples of Europe and the north descended, the wide spread of Japheth's line (9:27). The verse displays Japheth's sons, with the sons of Gomer (v.3).
3:
"And the sons of Gomer; Ashkenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah." The brief verse notes the sons of Gomer, the further peoples of the north. The grammatical voices note the line. Gill and Ellicott note that "the sons of Gomer; Ashkenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah" — the three sons of Gomer (himself a son of Japheth, v.2); from them descended further peoples of the north and Asia Minor; the genealogy traces the second generation from Japheth through Gomer.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the nations. Clarke and Gill note the peoples identified with Gomer's sons (by the ancient writers): Ashkenaz (a people of the north, associated with the region near Armenia, and later with the Germanic/northern peoples); Riphath (a northern people, perhaps near the Riphaean mountains); Togarmah (the Armenians, the house of Togarmah, a people of the north, Ezekiel 27:14; 38:6); the further spread of Japheth's line.

On the sons of Gomer, the grammatical-historical note the spread: from Gomer's sons came peoples of the north and Asia Minor; the genealogy shows the multiplying and spreading of Japheth's descendants into many nations; the peoples of the north traced through Gomer; the increase of the Gentile nations. The verse displays the sons of Gomer, the further peoples of the north.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "the sons of Gomer; Ashkenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah," the three sons of Gomer (Gill, Ellicott); the peoples — Ashkenaz, Riphath, Togarmah (the Armenians and northern peoples, Ezekiel 27:14; 38:6, Clarke, Gill); the further spread of Japheth's line through Gomer (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Gomer's line. The verse marks the sons: three. The verse displays the nations: of the north. The verse displays the Togarmah: Armenians. The verse displays the spread: continuing. The genealogy displays the further peoples of the north from Gomer. The verse displays the Gomer: his sons. The verse displays the peoples: descended. The verse displays the line: spreading. The sons of Gomer, the further peoples of the north, display the spread of Japheth's line: Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah — the sons of Gomer, from whom further peoples of the north and Asia Minor descended (Ezekiel 38:6), the multiplying of the Gentile nations. The verse displays Gomer's sons, with the sons of Javan (v.4).
4:
"And the sons of Javan; Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim." The brief verse notes the sons of Javan, the heads of the maritime and western peoples. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the sons of Javan; Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim" — the four sons of Javan (the head of the Greeks/Ionians, v.2); from them descended the maritime and western peoples, those of the isles and coasts; the genealogy traces the seafaring nations through Javan.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the nations. Clarke and Gill note the peoples identified with Javan's sons: Elishah (a people of Greece, perhaps Hellas/the Aeolians, or the coasts of the Aegean, Ezekiel 27:7); Tarshish (a maritime people, often identified with Tartessus in Spain, or a distant seaport, the ships of Tarshish); Kittim (the people of Cyprus, and the western isles/coasts, later the Romans/Macedonians); Dodanim (or Rodanim, the people of Rhodes, or a kindred Greek people); the heads of the maritime peoples.

On the sons of Javan, the grammatical-historical note the isles: from Javan's sons came the peoples of the isles and coasts (v.5), the seafaring nations of the west; the spread of Japheth's line over the islands and coastlands; the maritime peoples of the Mediterranean traced to Javan. The verse displays the sons of Javan, the heads of the maritime peoples.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "the sons of Javan; Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim," the four sons of Javan, head of the Greeks (Keil, Ellicott); the peoples — Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim (Cyprus), Dodanim (Rhodes) (Clarke, Gill); the maritime peoples of the isles traced to Javan (v.5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Javan's line. The verse marks the sons: four. The verse displays the nations: maritime. The verse displays the Kittim: Cyprus. The verse displays the Tarshish: seafaring. The genealogy displays the heads of the maritime peoples from Javan. The verse displays the Javan: his sons. The verse displays the isles: peopled. The verse displays the coasts: settled. The sons of Javan, the heads of the maritime peoples, display the spread of Japheth's line over the isles: Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim — the sons of Javan, from whom the seafaring peoples of the isles and coasts descended (v.5). The verse displays Javan's sons, dividing the isles of the Gentiles (v.5).
5:
"By these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands; every one after his tongue, after their families, in their nations." The witnesses note the division of the isles of the Gentiles among Japheth's descendants. On "By these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands," the grammatical voices note the spread. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "by these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands" — by the descendants of Japheth (vv.2-4) were the "isles of the Gentiles" (the coastlands and islands, especially of the Mediterranean and the west) divided and settled; the peoples of Japheth spread over the isles and coasts; the term "isles" embraces the maritime regions peopled by Japheth's line.

On "every one after his tongue," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the tongues. Wesley and Gill note that the division was "every one after his tongue" — the nations were distinguished by their languages; the diversity of tongues (anticipating, or referring to, the confusion at Babel, 11:7-9) marked the separation of the peoples; each nation had its own tongue.

On "after their families, in their nations," the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the order: "after their families, in their nations" — the peoples were divided by families, lands, tongues, and nations; the orderly spread and settlement of the nations; God's providence in dividing the earth among the peoples (Deuteronomy 32:8, "when the Most High divided to the nations their inheritance"); the formula (repeated for Ham, v.20, and Shem, v.31) marks the ordered distribution of the nations. The verse displays the division of the isles of the Gentiles among Japheth's descendants.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the spread — "by these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands," the coastlands settled by Japheth's descendants (Keil, Ellicott); "every one after his tongue," the nations distinguished by their languages (Babel, 11:7-9, Wesley, Gill); "after their families, in their nations," the ordered division of the peoples (Deuteronomy 32:8, JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the divided isles. The verse marks the isles: of the Gentiles. The verse displays the tongues: each its own. The verse displays the families: and nations. The verse displays the nations: ordered. The division of the isles of the Gentiles among Japheth's descendants displays the ordered spread of the nations: by these the isles of the Gentiles were divided, every one after his tongue, families, and nations — the coastlands settled by Japheth's line, the peoples distinguished by their tongues, the earth divided among the nations by God's providence (Deuteronomy 32:8). The verse displays the divided isles, before the sons of Ham (v.6).
6:
"And the sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, and Phut, and Canaan." The witnesses note the sons of Ham, the heads of the southern nations. On "the sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, and Phut, and Canaan," the grammatical voices note the line of Ham. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, and Phut, and Canaan" — the four sons of Ham; from them descended the peoples of the south (Africa) and parts of Arabia and Canaan; the genealogy traces the southern nations through Ham.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the nations. Clarke and Gill note the peoples identified with Ham's sons: Cush (Ethiopia, and parts of Arabia, the Cushites); Mizraim (Egypt — the name is the regular Hebrew word for Egypt); Phut (Libya, the Libyans of north Africa); Canaan (the Canaanites, the peoples of the land of Canaan); the heads of the southern nations and the Canaanites.

On the sons of Ham, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note Canaan: among Ham's sons is Canaan, whose descendants (vv.15-19) peopled the land of Canaan, on whom the curse fell (9:25); the note of Canaan (as in 9:18) connects the genealogy with the prophecy; the southern nations and the Canaanites traced to Ham; the peoples of Egypt, Ethiopia, Libya, and Canaan from Ham's line. The verse displays the sons of Ham, the heads of the southern nations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "the sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, and Phut, and Canaan," the four sons of Ham (Keil, Ellicott); the peoples — Cush (Ethiopia), Mizraim (Egypt), Phut (Libya), Canaan (the Canaanites) (Clarke, Gill); Canaan, on whom the curse fell (9:25), among Ham's sons (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Ham's line. The verse marks the sons: four. The verse displays the nations: southern. The verse displays the Egypt: Mizraim. The verse displays the Canaan: cursed. The genealogy displays the heads of the southern nations from Ham. The verse displays the Ham: his sons. The verse displays the peoples: Cush, Egypt, Libya, Canaan. The verse displays the line: spreading. The sons of Ham, the heads of the southern nations, display the line of Ham: Cush, Mizraim, Phut, and Canaan — the four sons from whom the peoples of Ethiopia, Egypt, Libya, and Canaan descended, including Canaan, on whom the curse fell (9:25). The verse displays Ham's sons, with the sons of Cush (v.7).
7:
"And the sons of Cush; Seba, and Havilah, and Sabtah, and Raamah, and Sabtecha: and the sons of Raamah; Sheba, and Dedan." The brief verse notes the sons of Cush, the peoples of Arabia and the south. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the sons of Cush; Seba, and Havilah, and Sabtah, and Raamah, and Sabtecha: and the sons of Raamah; Sheba, and Dedan" — the five sons of Cush (and two grandsons through Raamah); from them descended peoples of Arabia, the Red Sea region, and parts of Africa; the genealogy traces the Cushite peoples.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the nations. Clarke and Gill note the peoples identified with Cush's sons: Seba (a people of Africa/Arabia, Meroe or the Sabeans); Havilah (a region of Arabia); Sabtah, Sabtecha (peoples of Arabia); Raamah (a people of Arabia), and his sons Sheba and Dedan (Arabian peoples, the Sabeans and Dedanites, trading peoples of Arabia, Ezekiel 27:20-22); the peoples of Arabia and the Red Sea.

On the sons of Cush, the grammatical-historical note the spread: from Cush's sons came peoples of Arabia and the south; the Cushite line spread over Arabia and the regions of the Red Sea; the multiplying of Ham's descendants; the peoples of the south and Arabia traced through Cush. The verse displays the sons of Cush, the peoples of Arabia and the south.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "the sons of Cush; Seba, and Havilah, and Sabtah, and Raamah, and Sabtecha: and the sons of Raamah; Sheba, and Dedan," the sons and grandsons of Cush (Keil, Gill); the peoples — Seba, Havilah, Raamah, Sheba, Dedan (Arabian peoples, Ezekiel 27:20-22, Clarke, Gill); the Cushite peoples of Arabia and the Red Sea (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Cush's line. The verse marks the sons: five. The verse displays the grandsons: Sheba, Dedan. The verse displays the nations: Arabian. The verse displays the spread: south. The genealogy displays the peoples of Arabia from Cush. The verse displays the Cush: his sons. The verse displays the Arabia: peopled. The verse displays the line: spreading. The sons of Cush, the peoples of Arabia and the south, display the Cushite line: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, Sabtecha, and Raamah's sons Sheba and Dedan — the peoples of Arabia and the Red Sea descended from Cush (Ezekiel 27:20-22). The verse displays Cush's line, before Nimrod (v.8).
8:
"And Cush begat Nimrod: he began to be a mighty one in the earth." The witnesses note Nimrod, who began to be a mighty one in the earth — the first potentate. On "Cush begat Nimrod," the grammatical voices note the man. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Cush begat Nimrod" — Nimrod, a son (or descendant) of Cush, is singled out from the genealogy for special notice; he is the notable figure of the Table of Nations; the narrative pauses on Nimrod, the founder of the first kingdom (v.10).

On "he began to be a mighty one in the earth," interpreters expound the phrase. The puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note that Nimrod "began to be a mighty one (a gibbor, a mighty man, a hero) in the earth" — he was the first to rise to great power and dominion; he began the era of mighty men, conquerors, and rulers; "began to be" marks him as the first potentate, the founder of empire; whether by force, prowess, or tyranny, Nimrod made himself mighty and founded a kingdom (v.10).

On "a mighty one," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the power: Nimrod's might (whether admired or condemned, see v.9) marks the rise of human power and dominion after the flood; he gathered men under his rule and built the first kingdom (Babel and the cities of Shinar, v.10); the beginning of organized human empire, and (on the dominant reading) of tyranny and despotism; the first mighty one of the post-flood world. The verse displays Nimrod, who began to be a mighty one in the earth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the man — "Cush begat Nimrod," Nimrod singled out for special notice (Keil, Ellicott); "he began to be a mighty one in the earth," the first to rise to great power and dominion, the founder of empire (Henry, Calvin); the rise of human power and the first kingdom (v.10, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mighty Nimrod. The verse marks the Nimrod: begotten. The verse displays the might: a mighty one. The verse displays the beginning: of empire. The verse displays the kingdom: founded. The notice displays Nimrod the first potentate of the post-flood world. The verse displays the Nimrod: mighty. The verse displays the power: rising. The verse displays the empire: begun. Nimrod, who began to be a mighty one in the earth, displays the rise of human power and the first potentate: Cush begat Nimrod, who began to be a mighty one — the first to rise to great power and dominion, the founder of the first kingdom (v.10), the beginning of human empire (and, on the dominant reading, of tyranny). The verse displays the mighty Nimrod, the hunter before the LORD (v.9).
9:
"He was a mighty hunter before the LORD: wherefore it is said, Even as Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD." The witnesses note Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD — the crux of the phrase. On "He was a mighty hunter before the LORD," interpreters divide over the sense. The dominant reading (the reformed Gill, the grammatical-historical, and the ancient/Jewish tradition) takes the phrase in a bad sense: Nimrod was a "mighty hunter" not merely of beasts but of men — a violent conqueror, a tyrant who hunted and subdued men, seizing dominion by force; "before the LORD" is read as "against the LORD" or "in defiance of the LORD" (the name Nimrod, from marad, "he rebelled"; the Targums and Jewish tradition brand him as a rebel, apostate, and tyrant); the Jerusalem Targum and the rabbis make him the arch-rebel who founded a kingdom in defiance of God. A second, milder reading takes "mighty hunter before the LORD" in a more neutral or even favorable sense — a literal hunter of beasts (protecting men from wild animals), or one eminently mighty "in the sight of the LORD" (an idiom for exceeding greatness), without necessarily the note of rebellion.

On "before the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the idiom: "before the LORD" may mean "in the LORD's sight" (the superlative idiom, exceedingly mighty), or "against the LORD" (in open defiance); the dominant view, with the name and the Jewish tradition, takes it as marking Nimrod's God-defying tyranny. On "Even as Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD," the witnesses note that Nimrod became proverbial — a byword for a mighty, masterful, and (commonly) a tyrannical hunter and ruler; his name passed into a proverb for any powerful tyrant. The verse displays Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD.

The witnesses agree the substance: Nimrod was a mighty hunter and ruler, who became proverbial (Gill, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley). The phrase "before the LORD" bears a crux: a tyrant and rebel who hunted men in defiance of God ("before" = "against the LORD"; from marad, "he rebelled"; the dominant Jewish/traditional reading) vs a literal hunter or one eminently mighty "in the sight of the LORD" (the milder, neutral reading). The verse displays the Nimrod crux. The verse marks the hunter: mighty. The verse displays the debate: tyrant or hunter; against or before. The verse displays the proverb: even as Nimrod. The verse displays the defiance: of God. The notice displays Nimrod proverbial for masterful power. The verse displays the Nimrod: hunter. The verse displays the LORD: before/against. The verse displays the tyrant: branded. Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD displays the proverbial potentate and a crux: he was a mighty hunter before the LORD — read by the dominant view as a tyrant and rebel who hunted men in defiance of God (marad, "he rebelled"; the Jewish tradition), and by a milder view as a literal hunter or one eminently mighty in God's sight; his name a byword for any powerful tyrant. The verse displays the proverbial Nimrod, whose kingdom was Babel (v.10).
10:
"And the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar." The witnesses note the beginning of Nimrod's kingdom in the land of Shinar. On "the beginning of his kingdom was Babel," the grammatical voices note the first kingdom. Ellicott and Gill note that "the beginning of his kingdom was Babel" — Nimrod founded the first kingdom, and its beginning was Babel (Babylon); the first organized empire of the post-flood world; Babel, the city later associated with the tower and the confusion of tongues (ch. 11), was the seat of Nimrod's rule; the first kingdom of human dominion.

On "Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the cities. Clarke and Gill note that Nimrod's kingdom comprised four cities — Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh; the chief cities of his realm in the plain of Shinar (Babylonia/Mesopotamia); the centers of the first empire; the cities later known in the history of Babylonia.

On "in the land of Shinar," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the place: "in the land of Shinar" — the plain of Shinar (Babylonia, lower Mesopotamia), where the first kingdom was founded and where the tower of Babel would be built (11:2); the fertile plain that became the seat of the first empire; Nimrod's kingdom established in Shinar marks the beginning of empire and the place of the dispersion. The verse displays the beginning of Nimrod's kingdom in the land of Shinar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the kingdom — "the beginning of his kingdom was Babel," Nimrod founding the first kingdom at Babel (Ellicott, Gill); "Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh," the four chief cities of his realm (Clarke, Gill); "in the land of Shinar," the plain of Babylonia, seat of the first empire and the tower (11:2, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the first kingdom. The verse marks the kingdom: Nimrod's. The verse displays the Babel: its beginning. The verse displays the cities: four. The verse displays the Shinar: the land. The kingdom displays the first empire founded in Shinar. The verse displays the Babel: founded. The verse displays the empire: begun. The verse displays the Shinar: its seat. The beginning of Nimrod's kingdom in the land of Shinar displays the first empire: the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh, in Shinar — the first organized empire of the post-flood world, seated in the plain of Babylonia where the tower of Babel would rise (11:2). The verse displays Nimrod's kingdom, extended to Asshur (v.11).
11:
"Out of that land went forth Asshur, and builded Nineveh, and the city Rehoboth, and Calah," The witnesses note the building of Nineveh — the crux of whether Asshur or Nimrod went forth. On "Out of that land went forth Asshur," interpreters divide over the subject. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and many hold that the verse should read "out of that land he [Nimrod] went forth to Asshur (Assyria)" — Nimrod, from Shinar, extended his kingdom northward into Assyria and built Nineveh and the other cities; "Asshur" is taken as the place (Assyria) to which Nimrod went, not the person; the Targum of Jonathan and several read it of Nimrod: "out of that land went forth Nimrod and reigned... and built Nineveh." A second reading (the KJV text) takes "Asshur" as the person — Asshur (the son of Shem, v.22), going out of Shinar, built Nineveh and the cities of Assyria; on this view it is Shem's son Asshur, not Nimrod, who founded Nineveh.

On "builded Nineveh, and the city Rehoboth, and Calah," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the cities. JFB and Gill note that Nineveh (the great city, later the capital of Assyria) and the cities Rehoboth and Calah were built; the founding of the great cities of Assyria; whether by Nimrod (extending his empire) or by Asshur (founding his own), the cities of Assyria were established; Nineveh would become the famous capital of the Assyrian empire (Jonah; Nahum).

On the building, the grammatical-historical note the spread of empire: the cities of Assyria (Nineveh, Rehoboth, Calah) were founded as the kingdom spread northward from Shinar; whether Nimrod's empire reached into Assyria, or Asshur founded a kingdom there, the great cities of Mesopotamia arose; the spread of human dominion in the land of the two rivers. The verse displays the building of Nineveh and the cities of Assyria.

The witnesses agree the substance: Nineveh and the cities of Assyria were built as the empire spread from Shinar (Keil, JFB, Gill). The subject bears a crux: "out of that land he [Nimrod] went forth to Asshur (Assyria)" and built Nineveh — Nimrod extending his kingdom (the margin; Keil; the Targum) vs "out of that land went forth Asshur" (the son of Shem) and built Nineveh — Asshur founding the cities (the KJV text). The verse displays the Asshur crux. The verse marks the going-forth: from Shinar. The verse displays the debate: Nimrod or Asshur. The verse displays the Nineveh: built. The verse displays the cities: of Assyria. The building displays the cities of Assyria founded as empire spread. The verse displays the Asshur: or Nimrod. The verse displays the Nineveh: founded. The verse displays the empire: spreading. The building of Nineveh displays the spread of empire and a crux: out of that land Nineveh and the cities of Assyria were built — read either as Nimrod going forth to Assyria and extending his kingdom (Keil; the Targum), or as Asshur, son of Shem, going forth and founding Nineveh (the KJV text). The verse displays the built cities, with Resen (v.12).
12:
"And Resen between Nineveh and Calah: the same is a great city." The brief verse notes Resen, completing the cities of Assyria. The grammatical voices note the city. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Resen between Nineveh and Calah: the same is a great city" — Resen, a city lying between Nineveh and Calah, completes the list of the cities of Assyria (vv.11-12); "the same is a great city" — the description (whether of Resen, or of the whole complex of Nineveh, Rehoboth, Calah, and Resen taken together as the "great city") marks the greatness of the Assyrian metropolis.

On "between Nineveh and Calah," the witnesses note the location. Gill notes that Resen lay between Nineveh and Calah; the cluster of cities (Nineveh, Rehoboth, Calah, Resen) formed the great urban complex of Assyria; the chief cities of the Assyrian heartland.

On "the same is a great city," the grammatical-historical note the greatness: "the same is a great city" — whether Resen itself, or the whole cluster taken as one great city (the "great city" of Nineveh, embracing the surrounding towns, perhaps the "great city" of Jonah 3:3); the greatness of the Assyrian metropolis is noted; the rise of the great cities of empire after the flood; the urban centers of the first great kingdoms. The verse displays Resen, completing the cities of Assyria.

13:
"And Mizraim begat Ludim, and Anamim, and Lehabim, and Naphtuhim," The brief verse notes the sons of Mizraim, the peoples of Egypt and north Africa. The grammatical voices note the line. Gill and Clarke note that "Mizraim begat Ludim, and Anamim, and Lehabim, and Naphtuhim" — the peoples (named in the plural, as tribes/nations) descended from Mizraim (Egypt, v.6); from Mizraim came various peoples of Egypt and north Africa; the genealogy traces the Egyptian and Libyan peoples through Mizraim.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the peoples. Clarke and Gill note that the names (in the plural form) denote peoples or tribes: Ludim (a people, perhaps of north Africa or the Lydians of Egypt); Anamim, Lehabim (the Lehabim perhaps the Libyans), Naphtuhim (a people of Egypt); the tribes and peoples descended from Mizraim, settled in Egypt and north Africa.

On the sons of Mizraim, the grammatical-historical note the spread: from Mizraim came the peoples of Egypt and the neighboring regions; the Egyptian nation and its kindred peoples traced to Ham through Mizraim; the multiplying of Ham's line in the south; the peoples of Egypt and Libya descended from Mizraim. The verse displays the sons of Mizraim, the peoples of Egypt and north Africa.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 14:
"And Pathrusim, and Casluhim, (out of whom came Philistim,) and Caphtorim." The brief verse notes further peoples of Mizraim, including the origin of the Philistines. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Pathrusim, and Casluhim, (out of whom came Philistim,) and Caphtorim" — further peoples descended from Mizraim: Pathrusim (the people of Pathros, Upper Egypt); Casluhim and Caphtorim (peoples of Egypt or the neighboring coasts); the note "(out of whom came Philistim)" marks the origin of the Philistines from one of these peoples.

On "(out of whom came Philistim)," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the Philistines. Clarke and Gill note that the Philistines (Philistim) sprang from the Casluhim (or, by another reckoning, were connected with the Caphtorim, Caphtor; cf. Amos 9:7; Jeremiah 47:4); the origin of the Philistines, the later great enemies of Israel, is here traced to the line of Ham through Mizraim; the Philistines, a people of the Egyptian/Caphtorite stock, settled on the coast of Canaan.

On "and Caphtorim," the grammatical-historical note the Caphtorim: the Caphtorim (the people of Caphtor, perhaps Crete or the Egyptian coast) are connected with the origin of the Philistines (Deuteronomy 2:23; Amos 9:7); the peoples of the Egyptian and coastal stock; the origin of the Philistines noted in the genealogy of Ham. The verse displays further peoples of Mizraim, including the origin of the Philistines.

The verse displays the line of Mizraim, before the sons of Canaan (v.15).
15:
"And Canaan begat Sidon his firstborn, and Heth," The witnesses note the sons of Canaan, the heads of the Canaanite peoples. On "Canaan begat Sidon his firstborn," the grammatical voices note the line. Gill and Clarke note that "Canaan begat Sidon his firstborn" — Canaan (the son of Ham, v.6, on whom the curse fell, 9:25) begat Sidon, his firstborn; Sidon, the head of the Sidonians (Phoenicians), the founder of the great maritime city of Sidon; the firstborn of the Canaanite line.

On "Sidon his firstborn," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the Phoenicians. Henry and Gill note that Sidon, the firstborn, was the head of the Sidonians/Phoenicians, the great traders and seafarers of the Canaanite coast; the city of Sidon (and Tyre, her daughter) became famous; the Phoenician peoples descended from Canaan's firstborn.

On "and Heth," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the Hittites: "and Heth" — Heth, the head of the Hittites (the children of Heth, the Hittites of the land of Canaan, with whom Abraham later dealt, ch. 23); the Hittites, a great Canaanite people; the heads of the Canaanite nations begin with Sidon and Heth; the peoples on whom the curse (9:25) would fall, and whom Israel would later dispossess. The verse displays the sons of Canaan, the heads of the Canaanite peoples.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the line — "Canaan begat Sidon his firstborn, and Heth," Canaan's sons, the heads of the Canaanite peoples (Gill, Clarke); "Sidon his firstborn," the head of the Phoenicians (Henry, Gill); "and Heth," the head of the Hittites (ch. 23, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Canaan's sons, with the Jebusite and Amorite (v.16).
16:
"And the Jebusite, and the Amorite, and the Girgasite," The brief verse notes further Canaanite peoples descended from Canaan. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Jebusite, and the Amorite, and the Girgasite" — further Canaanite peoples descended from Canaan: the Jebusites (the people of Jebus, later Jerusalem); the Amorites (a great Canaanite people of the hill country and beyond Jordan); the Girgashites (a Canaanite people); the tribes of the land of Canaan, listed among the seven (or more) nations Israel would dispossess.

On "the Jebusite," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the peoples. Clarke and Gill note that the Jebusites held Jebus (Jerusalem) until David took it (2 Samuel 5); the Amorites were a powerful people (Sihon and Og, Amorite kings); the Girgashites a Canaanite tribe; the peoples named are the Canaanite nations of the land.

On the Canaanite peoples, the grammatical-historical note the nations of Canaan: the Jebusites, Amorites, and Girgashites are among the Canaanite nations (the "seven nations," Deuteronomy 7:1) that inhabited the land before Israel; the peoples of the cursed line of Canaan (9:25); the nations Israel would later dispossess in the conquest. The verse displays further Canaanite peoples descended from Canaan.

17:
"And the Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite," The brief verse notes further Canaanite peoples and northern tribes. The grammatical voices note the line. Gill and JFB note that "the Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite" — further peoples descended from Canaan: the Hivites (a Canaanite people, the Gibeonites being Hivites, Joshua 9); the Arkites (the people of Arka, a city of Phoenicia, north of Sidon); the Sinites (a people of the Phoenician coast or Lebanon); the Canaanite and Phoenician tribes of the north.

On "the Hivite," the witnesses note the peoples. Gill notes that the Hivites were a Canaanite people (the Gibeonites, Joshua 9; and the Shechemites, ch. 34); the Arkites and Sinites were northern Phoenician peoples, named from their cities; the tribes of the Canaanite north.

On the Canaanite peoples, the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the spread: the Hivites, Arkites, and Sinites, with the others, made up the many tribes of Canaan and Phoenicia; the Canaanite peoples spread along the coast and inland; the descendants of Canaan filling the land that would become the inheritance of Israel; the many nations of the cursed line. The verse displays further Canaanite peoples and northern tribes.

18:

The grammatical voices note the line. Gill notes that "the Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite" — the last of the Canaanite peoples named: the Arvadites (the people of Arvad, a Phoenician island-city); the Zemarites (a people of the Phoenician coast); the Hamathites (the people of Hamath, a city of Syria on the Orontes); the northern Canaanite and Syrian peoples, completing the list of Canaan's descendants.

On "the Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite," the witnesses note the peoples. Gill notes that these were northern Phoenician and Syrian peoples, named from their cities (Arvad, Zemar, Hamath); the tribes of the Canaanite and Syrian north; the completing of the eleven peoples descended from Canaan.

On "afterward were the families of the Canaanites spread abroad," the grammatical-historical note the spreading: "afterward were the families of the Canaanites spread abroad" — the Canaanite families spread out over the land (and beyond), settling the regions described in v.19; the dispersion and settlement of the many Canaanite peoples; the spreading of Canaan's line over the land that would become Israel's; the increase and distribution of the cursed line. The verse displays the last Canaanite tribes and their spreading abroad.

19:
"And the border of the Canaanites was from Sidon, as thou comest to Gerar, unto Gaza; as thou goest, unto Sodom, and Gomorrah, and Admah, and Zeboim, even unto Lasha." The brief verse notes the borders of the land of the Canaanites. The grammatical voices note the boundaries. Gill notes that "the border of the Canaanites was from Sidon... unto Gaza... unto Sodom, and Gomorrah, and Admah, and Zeboim, even unto Lasha" — the territory of the Canaanites is described by its borders: from Sidon (in the north) toward Gerar and Gaza (in the south-west), and toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboim (the cities of the plain, in the south-east) unto Lasha; the extent of the land the Canaanites occupied — the land that would become the promised land of Israel.

On "from Sidon... unto Gaza," the witnesses note the western border. Gill notes the line from Sidon in the north down the coast toward Gerar and Gaza; the western and southern extent of the Canaanite territory along the coast and the south.

On "unto Sodom, and Gomorrah, and Admah, and Zeboim," the grammatical-historical note the cities of the plain: the eastern/southern border ran toward the cities of the plain (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboim, later destroyed, ch. 19); the land of Canaan, bounded by these cities, is the land promised to Abraham (12:7; 15:18-21); the borders of the Canaanite land mark the inheritance Israel would receive; the territory of the cursed line that would pass to the chosen people. The verse displays the borders of the land of the Canaanites.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the borders — "the border of the Canaanites was from Sidon... unto Gaza... unto Sodom, and Gomorrah, and Admah, and Zeboim, even unto Lasha," the extent of the Canaanite land (Gill); "from Sidon... unto Gaza," the western/coastal border (Gill); "unto Sodom, and Gomorrah," the cities of the plain marking the border (ch. 19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Canaanite land, closing the sons of Ham (v.20).
20:
"These are the sons of Ham, after their families, after their tongues, in their countries, and in their nations." The brief verse notes the summary of the sons of Ham, in their families, tongues, and nations. The grammatical voices note the formula. Gill and Clarke note that "these are the sons of Ham, after their families, after their tongues, in their countries, and in their nations" — the summary formula (as for Japheth, v.5, and Shem, v.31) closes the genealogy of Ham; the descendants of Ham are distinguished by their families, tongues, countries, and nations; the ordered distribution of the Hamite peoples over the earth.

On "after their families, after their tongues," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the distinctions. Clarke and Gill note that the peoples were divided by families and tongues — each nation with its own kindred and language; the diversity of tongues (anticipating, or referring to, Babel, 11:7-9) marked the separation of the Hamite nations; the ordered distinction of the peoples.

On "in their countries, and in their nations," the grammatical-historical note the lands: "in their countries, and in their nations" — the Hamite peoples settled in their several countries and formed their nations; the southern nations (Egypt, Ethiopia, Libya, Canaan, and the Cushite peoples) distributed over their lands; God's providence in settling the nations in their territories (Deuteronomy 32:8; Acts 17:26); the ordered spread of Ham's line. The verse displays the summary of the sons of Ham.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the formula — "these are the sons of Ham, after their families, after their tongues, in their countries, and in their nations," the summary closing Ham's genealogy (Gill, Clarke); "after their families, after their tongues," the peoples distinguished by kindred and language (Babel, 11:7-9, Clarke, Gill); "in their countries, and in their nations," the peoples settled in their lands (Acts 17:26, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sons of Ham, before the sons of Shem (v.21).
21:
"Unto Shem also, the father of all the children of Eber, the brother of Japheth the elder, even to him were children born." The witnesses note Shem, the father of Eber's children and the line of promise. On "Unto Shem also, the father of all the children of Eber," the grammatical voices note the line of Shem. Gill and Calvin note that "Shem... the father of all the children of Eber" — Shem is named as the ancestor of Eber (from whom the Hebrews are named, and through whom the line of promise runs to Abraham); the genealogy of Shem is given last and with special honor, because the line of promise (and the Messiah) descends through Shem and Eber; Shem the father of the chosen line.

On "the father of all the children of Eber," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the Hebrews. Henry and Gill note that Eber (from whom "Hebrew" is derived) is singled out — Shem is "the father of all the children of Eber," the ancestor of the Hebrew people and the line of Abraham; the special notice of Eber marks the line of promise; the honor of Shem's genealogy is that it leads to the chosen people.

On "the brother of Japheth the elder," interpreters note the phrase. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the ambiguity: "the brother of Japheth the elder" may mean that Japheth was the elder brother (Japheth the eldest of Noah's sons), or that Shem was the elder; the Hebrew is variously rendered; most take Japheth as the elder, Shem being named first for the honor of the line of promise (not for primogeniture); the order of the genealogy follows the line of promise, not birth order. The verse displays Shem, the father of Eber's children and the line of promise.

The verse displays the line of Shem, with his sons (v.22).
22:
"The children of Shem; Elam, and Asshur, and Arphaxad, and Lud, and Aram." The brief verse notes the sons of Shem, the heads of the eastern nations. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the children of Shem; Elam, and Asshur, and Arphaxad, and Lud, and Aram" — the five sons of Shem; from them descended the peoples of the east and the line of promise; the genealogy traces the Semitic nations through Shem.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the nations. Clarke and Gill note the peoples identified with Shem's sons: Elam (the Elamites, the people of Elam/Persia, east of Babylonia); Asshur (the Assyrians, the people of Assyria — though see v.11 on Nineveh); Arphaxad (the ancestor of the line of promise, through Eber to Abraham, v.24); Lud (the Lydians, or a people of the east); Aram (the Aramaeans/Syrians); the heads of the eastern and Semitic nations.

The verse displays the sons of Shem, the heads of the eastern nations.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays Shem's sons, with the sons of Aram (v.23).
23:
"And the children of Aram; Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Mash." The brief verse notes the sons of Aram, the Aramaean peoples. The grammatical voices note the line. Gill and Clarke note that "the children of Aram; Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Mash" — the four sons of Aram (the head of the Aramaeans/Syrians, v.22); from them descended Aramaean peoples of Syria and the regions to the east; the genealogy traces the Aramaean tribes through Aram.

On the names, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the peoples. Clarke and Gill note the peoples identified with Aram's sons: Uz (the land of Uz, the home of Job, Job 1:1; a region of the Aramaean/Arabian borderlands); Hul, Gether, and Mash (Aramaean peoples of Syria and the eastern regions, variously located); the Aramaean tribes of the north and east.

The verse displays the sons of Aram, the Aramaean peoples.

25:
"And unto Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg; for in his days was the earth divided; and his brother's name was Joktan." The witnesses note Peleg, in whose days the earth was divided — the crux of the division. On "unto Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg," the grammatical voices note the sons. Gill and Ellicott note that "unto Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg... and his brother's name was Joktan" — Eber had two sons, Peleg (through whom the line of promise continues to Abraham, 11:16-26) and Joktan (the ancestor of various Arabian peoples, vv.26-29); the genealogy divides here into the line of Peleg (the promise) and the line of Joktan (the Arabian tribes).

On "Peleg; for in his days was the earth divided," interpreters divide over the meaning of the division. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and the dominant reading take it as the division of the earth among the nations — "the earth divided" refers to the dispersion of mankind at Babel (11:1-9), the confusion of tongues and the scattering of the peoples into separate nations, which happened in Peleg's days (his name, Peleg, meaning "division," commemorating the event); a political/national division of the earth among the peoples. A second reading (the wesleyan-arminian Clarke) takes it as a physical division — the separation of continents and islands from the mainland (a literal geographical or geological division of the earth's surface) in Peleg's days; on this view a physical, not a political, division is meant.

On "his brother's name was Joktan," the witnesses note the Arabian line: Joktan, the brother of Peleg, was the ancestor of many Arabian tribes (vv.26-29); the line of Joktan peopled Arabia; while the line of Peleg carried the promise; the two brothers heading the two branches. The verse displays Peleg, in whose days the earth was divided.

32:
